notes: 4 clues per country, ordered hardest/most obscure -> most obvious, replacing the old auto-generated hemisphere/size/border clues.

clues: Its capital was carved out of land donated by two states and belongs to no state at all.
It's home to the world's first national park, established in 1872.
It's the third-largest country in the world by population.
Its flag has 50 stars, one for each state.
It has more lakes than the rest of the world's lakes combined.
Its capital was chosen partly because it sat safely between two rival cities, Toronto and Quebec City.
It's the second-largest country in the world by area, despite a relatively small population.
Its flag features a maple leaf.
Its capital was built atop the ruins of the Aztec city of Tenochtitlan.
It gave the world chocolate, chili peppers, and corn.
It has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country in the Americas.
Its flag features an eagle eating a snake atop a cactus.
It's home to Tikal, one of the largest ancient Maya cities ever discovered.
It's one of the world's top exporters of cardamom, a spice more associated with India.
Its name is thought to come from a Nahuatl word meaning 'place of many trees.'
It's the most populous country in Central America.
It's the largest island in the Caribbean.
Classic American cars from the 1950s are still commonly driven here today.
It's known worldwide for cigars and son-based music styles that shaped salsa.
It lies just about 90 miles south of Florida.
Its name is Spanish for 'depths,' possibly referring to deep coastal waters Columbus encountered.
It's home to the ancient Maya ruins of Copán.
It has coastline on the Caribbean Sea but not the Pacific Ocean mainland proper.
Its capital's name comes from an indigenous word meaning 'silver hill.'
It's home to Lake Nicaragua, one of the only freshwater lakes in the world with sharks.
It's the largest country in Central America by land area.
Its capital was chosen as a compromise between two rival cities, León and Granada.
It's known for volcanoes, including one that people can board down.
It abolished its military entirely in 1948.
It contains about 5% of the world's biodiversity despite its small size.
Its name is Spanish for 'rich coast.'
It's a top destination for eco-tourism in Central America.
It's the narrowest country in Central America, home to a famous man-made shortcut for ships.
Its canal took over a decade to complete and reshaped global shipping routes.
It's the only place in the world where you can see the sun rise on the Pacific and set on the Atlantic from certain points.
It connects Central America to South America.
It shares its island with Haiti, the only island in the world split between two countries.
Its capital is the oldest continuously inhabited European-founded city in the Americas.
It's the birthplace of merengue music.
It occupies the eastern two-thirds of the island of Hispaniola.
It was the first country in the Americas to abolish slavery and gain independence from a European colonial power, in 1804.
It shares its island with the Dominican Republic.
French and Haitian Creole are its two official languages.
It occupies the western third of the island of Hispaniola.
It's the only country in Central America where English is the official language.
It's home to the Great Blue Hole, a massive underwater sinkhole visible from space.
Its capital was built inland after a hurricane destroyed the previous capital in 1961.
It was formerly known as British Honduras.
Its capital was built from scratch in the 1950s and shaped like an airplane from above.
It's home to more of the Amazon rainforest than any other country.
It's the largest country in South America by both size and population.
It's the only Portuguese-speaking country in the Americas.
It's home to Ushuaia, often called the southernmost city in the world.
Its name comes from the Latin word for silver, argentum.
It's the birthplace of tango.
It's the second-largest country in South America.
It's the longest north-south country in the world, stretching over 2,600 miles.
It's home to the driest non-polar desert on Earth, the Atacama.
Easter Island, famous for its stone statues, is part of its territory.
It hugs the western edge of South America along the Andes.
It's home to Machu Picchu, the famous lost Inca city.
It's considered one of the origin points of the potato, with thousands of native varieties still grown there.
Its cuisine, ceviche, is one of its most famous culinary exports.
The ancient Inca Empire was centered here.
It's the only South American country with coastlines on both the Pacific and the Caribbean.
It's the world's second-largest coffee producer.
It's named after Christopher Columbus.
It's known for its emeralds, some of the finest in the world.
It's home to Angel Falls, the tallest waterfall in the world.
It has some of the largest proven oil reserves on Earth.
Its name is Spanish for 'little Venice,' inspired by stilt houses early explorers saw.
It sits along South America's northern Caribbean coast.
It's named for the imaginary line that runs directly through it.
The Galápagos Islands, famous for inspiring Darwin's theory of evolution, belong to it.
Its capital sits at one of the highest elevations of any national capital in the world.
It straddles the equator on South America's Pacific coast.
It has two capital cities; one hosts the government, the other the judiciary.
It's home to the Salar de Uyuni, the world's largest salt flat.
It's one of only two landlocked countries in South America.
It's named after Simón Bolívar, the independence leader.
It's one of only two landlocked countries in South America.
Guaraní, an indigenous language, is an official language alongside Spanish.
It's home to one of the largest hydroelectric dams in the world, shared with Brazil.
It sits between Argentina, Brazil, and Bolivia.
It's one of the most secular countries in Latin America, having separated church and state early.
It was the first country in the world to legalize marijuana nationwide.
It's one of the smallest countries in South America by area.
It sits between Argentina and Brazil along the Atlantic coast.
It's the only country in South America where English is the official language.
Its name means 'land of many waters' in an indigenous language.
Recent offshore oil discoveries have made it one of the fastest-growing economies in the world.
It was formerly a British colony known as British Guiana.
It's the smallest sovereign country in South America.
It's the only country in the Americas where Dutch is the official language.
More than half of its land is covered in untouched rainforest.
It was formerly a Dutch colony known as Dutch Guiana.
